Refrens alternative

A Refrens alternative for businesses that need accounts preparation beyond invoicing.

Refrens is known for invoicing, GST billing, payments, compliance, and business workflows. Fastbooks focuses on the capture layer before final accounts: bills, receipts, bank rows, and voice notes become review-ready accounting drafts.

Get Early Access on WhatsApp

We respect your privacy. Message opens directly in WhatsApp; no info is saved on this site.

See WhatsApp accounting

Direct answer

Is Fastbooks a Refrens alternative?

Fastbooks covers a different workflow. While Refrens handles clean document creation (invoices, quotes, and payment gateways), Fastbooks focus on turning unstructured inputs into review-ready accounting drafts. It prepares transaction listings from bills, receipts, bank rows, or voice notes, allowing accountants to review treatment before official entry posting.

  • Capture expenses, bank rows, and cash context on the go.
  • Drafts are prepared before final document conversion.
  • Ideal for teams that want review-ready entries rather than only invoicing.

The Entry Preparation Angle

Invoicing is one part of accounts. Daily entry preparation is the harder part.

Many businesses can create invoices. The harder work is keeping the whole accounts trail clean: purchases, expenses, receipts, payments, bank narrations, cash movements, outstanding follow-up, and GST review. Fastbooks is built for this preparation layer. It helps collect business context from everyday inputs and prepares structured drafts for accountant approval.

Workflow comparison

Refrens-style invoicing workflow vs Fastbooks entry-preparation workflow

Aspect Refrens-style invoicing workflow Fastbooks entry-preparation workflow
Main strength Invoicing, billing, payments, compliance, and cloud business workflows. Accounting entry preparation from everyday business inputs.
Starting point Create or manage invoices and business documents. Send bill photos, receipts, bank rows, or voice notes.
Accounts preparation Accounting features are tied to structured business documents. Draft entries can start from messy real-world inputs before they become clean records.
Service-business fit Strong fit for invoicing-led service and professional businesses. Strong fit where the problem is daily capture, accountant review, and manual entry reduction.
Review philosophy Workflow depends on how the business manages records. AI prepares; accountant/human approves.
Best fit Businesses wanting a polished invoicing and compliance suite. Businesses wanting a capture-first review inbox for accounting drafts.

Frictionless Flow

How Fastbooks addresses the messy first mile

When Refrens-style invoicing is enough

If your main need is creating polished invoices, quotations, and payment records, an invoicing-led tool may work well.

When Fastbooks is the sharper fit

If your staff sends bills on WhatsApp, expenses are remembered later, and bank narrations need accountant interpretation, Fastbooks targets that workflow directly.

Built for accountant review

Fastbooks does not need to pretend that every AI suggestion is correct. It should prepare drafts and show uncertainty clearly.

Honest fit

Fastbooks may not be the right fit if:

You need simple billing or ERP

  • You only need simple invoice creation.
  • You want a mature full-suite accounting system today.

You want hands-off automation

  • You want a fully automatic posting system with no review.
  • Your current accounting team does not want a review-first workflow.

FAQ

Refrens alternative questions

Is Fastbooks better than Refrens?

Not in every case. Refrens-style tools are strong for invoicing and business workflows. Fastbooks is positioned for accounting entry preparation from everyday inputs and accountant review.

Is Fastbooks only an invoicing tool?

No. The Fastbooks direction is broader: sales, purchase, expense, bank, receipt, payment, outstanding, and GST-review draft preparation.

Who should consider Fastbooks instead of Refrens?

Businesses where daily accounts preparation is painful: many bills, receipts, bank rows, staff notes, cash expenses, and accountant follow-ups.
